notes.note: A recommendation for L. Aelius Lamia to Q. Cornificius, proconsul of Africa. sal. is salutem (dicit), the standard "sends greetings" of a familiar letter.

notes.anchor: he was banished by the consul A. Gabinius
notes.note: In 58 BC the consul A. Gabinius, ally of Clodius, ordered Lamia (a leading knight) out of Rome by edict (relegatio) for openly agitating on Cicero's behalf during Cicero's exile.

notes.anchor: It was witnessed by a great audience
notes.note: Cicero casts Lamia's loyalty as a public spectacle (magno theatro, "in a great theatre"), a stock image for conduct done before the eyes of the whole citizenry.

notes.anchor: every kind of falsified decree of the Senate was being put about
notes.note: After Caesar's death Antony was accused of forging senatus consulta and inserting bogus measures among Caesar's papers; Cicero exploits this to deny that any decree damaging Cornificius was genuinely Lamia's doing.
A particular senatorial decree associated with a Sempronius, evidently one Cornificius suspected was aimed at him; Cicero denies any part in it, noting he was not even at Rome when it passed.
Cura ut valeas, the conventional sign-off of a Roman letter, equivalent to "farewell" / "keep well."
